---
title: "Typst Page Layout Test"
short-title: "Page Layout Test"
course-label: "STAT 201 Inference"
document-context: "Bayes | Fa 2026"
footer-logo: "../../logos/m-symbol-black.png"
format: typst
---

## Purpose

This document tests the shared Typst page layout used for teaching and learning materials.

Check the rendered PDF for the following:

-   The short document title should be hidden on page 1.
-   The short document title should appear in the upper-left header starting on page 2.
-   The course label should appear in the upper-right header on every page.
-   The footer should include instructor and semester information on the left.
-   The page number should appear in the center footer.
-   The logo should appear in the lower-right footer.
-   Header and footer rules should not collide with body text.

## Sample context

This handout represents a generic instructional document. It is not intended to test exam-specific formatting, worksheet answer boxes, rubric tables, or other specialized document components. Its purpose is only to check whether the shared page layout behaves correctly across multiple pages.

A useful page layout should remain stable when the document includes ordinary teaching material: headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, code blocks, and explicit page breaks. The layout should also remain readable when a page contains dense text or a heading near the top or bottom of the page.

## Learning objectives

After using this test file, the maintainer should be able to verify that the shared page layout:

1.  Applies consistently across a multi-page PDF.
2.  Suppresses redundant running-title text on the first page.
3.  Displays running headers and footers on later pages.
4.  Leaves enough body space for ordinary document content.
5.  Provides a reusable pattern for course-specific Quarto documents.

## Example instructions

Read the scenario below and identify the important parts of the document layout. This text is filler, but it resembles the length and structure of an ordinary teaching handout.

Students often work with documents that include a title, short overview, numbered steps, examples, tables, and short interpretation prompts. A stable page layout should support all of these elements without requiring custom formatting in each document.

### Things to inspect

-   Is the header visually separated from the body?
-   Is the footer visually separated from the body?
-   Does the page number stay centered?
-   Does the logo stay aligned to the right?
-   Does the body text have enough vertical room?
-   Does the second-page running title appear as expected?

## Example table

| Item | What to check | Expected result |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Header left | Short document title | Hidden on page 1, visible on page 2+ |
| Header right | Course label | Visible on every page |
| Footer left | Instructor and semester | Visible on every page |
| Footer center | Page number | Centered on every page |
| Footer right | Logo | Visible if a valid image path is provided |

## Example code block

``` r
library(tidyverse)

example_data <- tibble(
  group = c("A", "A", "B", "B"),
  value = c(12, 15, 18, 21)
)

example_data |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarize(mean_value = mean(value))
```

## Longer sample text

This section adds enough content to push the document onto additional pages. The goal is to check whether the running header and footer continue to behave predictably after the first page.

A handout often needs enough structure to guide students through a task without overwhelming them. It may include short sections, lists of steps, interpretation prompts, reminders, and small tables. The formatting should support the instructional purpose without becoming visually distracting.

A reusable page layout is useful because it separates routine document structure from the content of individual teaching materials. The document author should not need to re-create page headers, footers, page numbers, or logo placement in every file. Instead, each document should pass a small number of values into the shared layout function.

This approach also makes maintenance easier. If the footer logo needs to change, or the header spacing needs adjustment, the change can be made once in the shared Typst file and then reused across course repositories.

## Second-page behavior

This section should appear on page 2 or later after rendering. Confirm that the short document title appears in the upper-left header on this page.

The first page already includes the full title generated by Quarto. Repeating the short title in the header on page 1 is usually redundant. On later pages, however, the short title helps identify the document when pages are printed, separated, or viewed out of order.

## More sample content

Use this section to check whether ordinary paragraphs sit comfortably between the header and footer.

A well-designed course document should be readable when printed on letter-sized paper. Margins should leave room for binding, annotation, and normal printer limitations. Headers and footers should provide useful context without taking too much space away from the main content.

The same layout should work for public student-facing documents and private instructor-facing materials. The content may differ, but the page structure can remain consistent.

## Explicit page break

## Page after forced break

This section follows an explicit page break. Confirm that the header and footer remain consistent after the break.

Forced page breaks are common in handouts, exams, answer keys, and lab instructions. The page layout should remain stable when a document author intentionally starts a new page.

## Final checklist

Before accepting changes to `page-layout.typ`, check the rendered PDF manually.

-   Page 1 does not show the short document title in the upper-left header.
-   Page 2 and later pages show the short document title in the upper-left header.
-   The course label appears in the upper-right header.
-   The document-context text appears consistently.
-   Page numbers are centered.
-   The logo renders correctly.
-   No header or footer content overlaps the body.
-   The document still looks acceptable when printed.
